Literature: Saint Martin had 2 recorded plague outbreaks, in 1550 and 1551.
Cultural_Significance: London suffered more plague outbreaks than any other English city, with recorded episodes spanning from 1348 to the Great Plague of 1665-66. As England's largest city and primary port, London was both the most exposed and the most densely populated place in the country. The city's crowded parishes, poor sanitation and constant flow of goods and people made containment almost impossible. The Great Plague of 1665 killed an estimated 100,000 Londoners, roughly a quarter of the city's population, and was the last major outbreak of bubonic plague in England.

Literature: Saint Nicholas had 1 recorded plague outbreak, in 1594.
Cultural_Significance: London suffered more plague outbreaks than any other English city, with recorded episodes spanning from 1348 to the Great Plague of 1665-66. As England's largest city and primary port, London was both the most exposed and the most densely populated place in the country. The city's crowded parishes, poor sanitation and constant flow of goods and people made containment almost impossible. The Great Plague of 1665 killed an estimated 100,000 Londoners, roughly a quarter of the city's population, and was the last major outbreak of bubonic plague in England.

Literature: Kirkton of Menmuir had 1 recorded plague outbreak, in 1647.
Cultural_Significance: Scotland suffered devastating plague outbreaks from the mid-14th century onwards, with Edinburgh, Aberdeen, Perth and Dundee among the most affected towns. Scottish plague history is characterised by repeated epidemics arriving via trade routes from continental Europe and England, with outbreaks often following periods of political instability and famine. Edinburgh recorded over 30 separate outbreak events, making it one of the most plague-afflicted cities in the British Isles.

Literature: Saint Katherines had 1 recorded plague outbreak, in 1638.
Cultural_Significance: London suffered more plague outbreaks than any other English city, with recorded episodes spanning from 1348 to the Great Plague of 1665-66. As England's largest city and primary port, London was both the most exposed and the most densely populated place in the country. The city's crowded parishes, poor sanitation and constant flow of goods and people made containment almost impossible. The Great Plague of 1665 killed an estimated 100,000 Londoners, roughly a quarter of the city's population, and was the last major outbreak of bubonic plague in England.

Literature: St Mary's had 3 recorded plague outbreaks, in 1584, 1585 and 1604.
Cultural_Significance: The Black Death reached Orkney in 1349–1350, causing severe mortality across the islands. It probably arrived through maritime trade from mainland Scotland or Norway. Contemporary Icelandic sources record the epidemic in Orkney, although no specific outbreak is recorded for the village of St Mary's (Holm).  Large areas of farmland remained abandoned for generations afterwards, suggesting substantial population loss.
